name: Coin-cell environment sensor
description: A sensor that sleeps almost all the time and wakes to take a reading. The whole design is a fight against average current: the radio and the sensor are awake for milliseconds, and everything else is sleep.

suggestedBlocks.name: MCU
suggestedBlocks.hint: Pick for sleep current, not speed — it sets the floor the whole design sits on.

suggestedBlocks.name: Environment sensor
suggestedBlocks.hint: Measures on demand, then returns to sleep. Forced mode beats continuous.

suggestedBlocks.name: Regulator
suggestedBlocks.hint: The regulator's quiescent current flows 24/7 — it is the floor under everything. A 55 uA LDO alone caps a 220 mAh coin cell at ~5 months; this design needs roughly 1 uA of quiescent draw or none (run direct from the cell if the parts allow it).

bringup: Measure sleep current before anything else — it decides the lifetime
Confirm the sensor actually returns to sleep after a reading

bringUpChecklist.text: Power the board from a bench supply set to the coin-cell voltage and confirm quiescent current matches the budget
bringUpChecklist.hint: A bench supply lets you read current directly; a coin cell does not.

bringUpChecklist.text: Confirm the MCU enumerates on the debug probe
bringUpChecklist.hint: If the probe cannot see the chip, nothing downstream of this matters yet.

bringUpChecklist.text: Blink an LED before trusting any peripheral
bringUpChecklist.hint: Proves the toolchain, the flash, and the clock are all working before you debug a sensor on top of them.

bringUpChecklist.text: Measure sleep current with a multimeter in series, not just estimated from the datasheet
bringUpChecklist.hint: The datasheet number is a best case; board leakage and unused pins routinely add microamps that decide the lifetime.

name: LoRa mesh node
description: A long-range radio node. Transmit is a brief, brutal current spike inside a much longer listening window — the two must be budgeted separately or the estimate is nonsense.

suggestedBlocks.name: MCU
suggestedBlocks.hint: Needs SPI for the radio and a low-power stop mode between packets.

suggestedBlocks.name: LoRa radio
suggestedBlocks.hint: TX draws ~100 mA but only for the airtime of a packet; RX is smaller and far longer.

suggestedBlocks.name: Regulator
suggestedBlocks.hint: Must survive the TX current step without the rail sagging — bulk capacitance matters here.

bringup: Never power the radio without an antenna or a 50 Ω load attached
Check the rail with a scope during TX, not just a multimeter

bringUpChecklist.text: Power the board from a bench supply and confirm quiescent current before the first TX
bringUpChecklist.hint: Establishes the resting current so a later TX spike is measured against a known baseline.

bringUpChecklist.text: Confirm the MCU enumerates on the debug probe
bringUpChecklist.hint: If the probe cannot see the chip, nothing downstream of this matters yet.

bringUpChecklist.text: Blink an LED before trusting any peripheral
bringUpChecklist.hint: Proves the toolchain, the flash, and the clock are all working before you debug the radio on top of them.

bringUpChecklist.text: Attach an antenna or a 50 ohm dummy load before ever keying the radio
bringUpChecklist.hint: Transmitting into an open SPI/RF pin with no load can damage the power amplifier immediately.

bringUpChecklist.text: Watch the rail on a scope during a TX burst, not just a multimeter
bringUpChecklist.hint: A multimeter averages the reading away; the sag that browns out the MCU only shows up on a scope.

name: E-ink weather console
description: A display that costs nothing to keep showing an image and a lot to change it. Power is dominated by refresh rate — how often you update is the whole design decision.

suggestedBlocks.name: MCU
suggestedBlocks.hint: Wakes, fetches, redraws, sleeps. Wi-Fi parts are convenient but hurt the average.

suggestedBlocks.name: E-ink display
suggestedBlocks.hint: Draws current only while refreshing — holding an image is free.

suggestedBlocks.name: Environment sensor
suggestedBlocks.hint: Local temperature and humidity to pair with the forecast.

bringup: Never leave an e-ink panel mid-refresh — it can ghost or damage the film

name: Rocket avionics
description: Flies once, for a minute, and must not fail. Power is not the constraint — the pyro current spike, the brownout it can cause, and never firing a channel by accident are.

suggestedBlocks.name: Flight computer
suggestedBlocks.hint: Logs continuously through the flight — no sleeping here.

suggestedBlocks.name: Barometer
suggestedBlocks.hint: Apogee detection. Sample fast — this is the one sensor that decides the flight.

suggestedBlocks.name: IMU
suggestedBlocks.hint: Orientation and launch detection; the accelerometer confirms liftoff.

suggestedBlocks.name: Pyro channel
suggestedBlocks.hint: Amps for milliseconds. The spike browns out everything else unless there is bulk capacitance.

bringup: Test continuity with a lamp, never a live charge
Confirm the pyro output is low through power-up and reset — not just in the main loop

name: Motion control
description: A driven axis. The motor is the load and the noise source: stall current sets the supply, and the driver's heat sets the enclosure.

suggestedBlocks.name: MCU
suggestedBlocks.hint: Needs timers for step/dir or PWM, and to stay responsive under load.

suggestedBlocks.name: Motor driver
suggestedBlocks.hint: Size for STALL current, not running current — stall is what you actually hit.

suggestedBlocks.name: Regulator
suggestedBlocks.hint: Keep the logic rail clean and away from the motor rail's switching noise.

bringup: Current-limit the supply before the first move
Check driver temperature after a stall test
